title: The Hidden Attack! Move One, Attack With Another!

root.narration: Your knight on e5 is sitting right in front of your bishop on c4. The bishop is aiming at f7 -- the weak pawn next to the enemy king! If you move the knight out of the way, your bishop will attack f7. This is called a discovered attack. But where should your knight jump to cause the most damage?

choices.text: Jump the knight to d7, forking the queen on d8 and the bishop on f8! When the knight moves, your bishop is revealed attacking f7. TWO threats at once!
Move the knight back to d3. It gets out of the way and the bishop is revealed.
Push a pawn. You do not want to move the knight right now.

outcome-great.narration: Your knight leaps to d7, forking the queen and bishop! At the same time, moving the knight REVEALED your bishop on c4, which now attacks the weak f7 pawn. Your opponent cannot deal with all these threats at once! They save the queen but lose the bishop on f8, and your bishop threatens f7. Brilliant!

outcome.headline: DISCOVERED ATTACK! Two Threats at Once!
outcome.explanation: A discovered attack is one of the most powerful tactics in chess. You move one piece, and that reveals an attack by a DIFFERENT piece behind it. The key is to make the piece you move ALSO create a threat. That gives your opponent TWO problems to deal with at once! It is like a double attack, but even sneakier because the second attack was hidden. The piece you move should threaten something valuable too.
outcome.whatToRemember: When you can uncover an attack, make sure the piece you move ALSO creates a threat. Two threats are better than one!

keyTerms.definition: Moving one piece out of the way to reveal an attack by a piece behind it. Like opening a curtain to show a surprise!
A special discovered attack where the revealed piece gives check to the king. Extra powerful because the king MUST move!

outcome-okay.narration: You move the knight back to d3, uncovering your bishop. The bishop on c4 now attacks f7, which is good. But the knight on d3 is not threatening anything special. You only created one threat instead of two. Your opponent can just defend f7 and everything is fine for them.

outcome.headline: Discovered Attack, But Only Half Power!
outcome.explanation: You got the discovery part right -- moving the knight revealed the bishop attacking f7. But the knight went to a quiet square that does not threaten anything. In a discovered attack, the piece you move should ALSO create a threat. If you jump the knight to d7 instead, it forks the queen and bishop AND the bishop threatens f7. Multiple threats at once is much harder to defend!
outcome.whatToRemember: In a discovered attack, make the moving piece also threaten something. Create two problems, not just one!

keyTerms.definition: Attacking two things at once. The more threats you create in one move, the harder it is for your opponent!

outcome-bad.narration: You push a pawn instead of using the discovered attack. Nothing exciting happens. Your queen stays blocked behind the knight and you miss a chance to create two threats at once. Your opponent gets time to improve their position.

outcome.headline: Discovery Missed! Keep Your Eyes Open!
outcome.explanation: When one of your pieces is blocking another, think about what happens if you move it out of the way. That is a discovered attack waiting to happen! Your knight was blocking your bishop from attacking f7. By moving the knight to d7 (forking queen and bishop), you would have unleashed the bishop AND created a knight fork. Multiple attacks for the price of one move! Always check for discovered attacks -- they are sneaky and powerful.
outcome.whatToRemember: If one of your pieces is in front of another, think about discovered attacks! Move the front piece to create TWO threats!

keyTerms.definition: Two pieces lined up on the same line, like a queen behind a bishop or a rook behind a queen. They work together!
